**This article contains [chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm") characters used to write [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), as well as [sawndip](/wiki/Sawndip "Sawndip") characters used to write [Zhuang](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(East_Asian) "Help:Multilingual support (East Asian)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)").

**Chinese characters**[[a]](#cite_note-lead-2) are [logographs](/wiki/Logograph "Logograph") used [to write the Chinese languages](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") and others from regions historically influenced by [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture"), including [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"). Of the four independently invented [writing systems](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") accepted by scholars (cuneiform, Egyptian hieroglyphs, Chinese, Mesoamerican), they represent the only one that has remained in continuous use. Over a documented history spanning more than three millennia, the function, style, and means of writing characters have changed greatly. Unlike letters in alphabets that reflect the sounds of speech, Chinese characters generally represent [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme"), the units of meaning in a language. Writing all of the frequently used vocabulary in a language requires roughly 2000–3000 characters; as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit), more than 100000 Chinese characters have been identified and included in *[The Unicode Standard](/wiki/The_Unicode_Standard "The Unicode Standard")*. Characters are created according to several principles, where aspects of shape and pronunciation may be used to indicate the character's meaning.

The first attested characters are [oracle bone inscriptions](/wiki/Oracle_bone_inscriptions "Oracle bone inscriptions") made during the 13th century BCE in what is now [Anyang](/wiki/Anyang "Anyang"), Henan, as part of divinations conducted by the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") royal house. Character forms were originally [ideographic](/wiki/Ideograph "Ideograph") or [pictographic](/wiki/Pictograph "Pictograph") in style, but evolved as writing spread across China. Numerous attempts have been made to reform the script, including the promotion of [small seal script](/wiki/Small_seal_script "Small seal script") by the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BCE). [Clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script"), which had matured by the early [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BCE – 220 CE), abstracted the forms of characters—obscuring their pictographic origins in favour of making them easier to write. Following the Han, [regular script](/wiki/Regular_script "Regular script") emerged as the result of cursive influence on clerical script, and has been the primary style used for characters since. Informed by a long tradition of [lexicography](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography"), states using Chinese characters have standardized their forms—broadly, [simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_characters "Simplified characters") are used to write Chinese in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), while [traditional characters](/wiki/Traditional_characters "Traditional characters") are used in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau").

Where the use of characters spread beyond China, they were initially used to write [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese"); they were then often adapted to write local languages spoken throughout the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere"). In [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"), and [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), Chinese characters are known as *[kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji")*, *[hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja")*, and *[chữ Hán](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_H%C3%A1n "Chữ Hán")* respectively. Writing traditions also emerged for some of the other [languages of China](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China"), like the [sawndip](/wiki/Sawndip "Sawndip") script used to write the [Zhuang languages](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages") of [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"). Each of these written vernaculars used existing characters to write the language's native vocabulary, as well as the [loanwords it borrowed from Chinese](/wiki/Sino-Xenic_vocabularies "Sino-Xenic vocabularies"). In addition, each invented characters for local use. In written Korean and Vietnamese, Chinese characters have largely been replaced with alphabets—leaving Japanese as the only major non-Chinese language still written using them, alongside the other elements of the [Japanese writing system](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system").

At the most basic level, characters are composed of [strokes](/wiki/Chinese_character_strokes "Chinese character strokes") that are written in a fixed order. Historically, methods of writing characters have included inscribing stone, bone, or bronze; brushing ink onto silk, bamboo, or paper; and printing with [woodblocks](/wiki/Woodblock_printing "Woodblock printing") or [moveable type](/wiki/Moveable_type "Moveable type"). Technologies invented since the 19th century to facilitate the use of characters include [telegraph codes](/wiki/Telegraph_code "Telegraph code") and [typewriters](/wiki/Typewriter "Typewriter"), as well as [input methods](/wiki/Input_method "Input method") and [text encodings](/wiki/Text_encodings "Text encodings") on computers.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Development")]

Further information: [Proto-writing](/wiki/Proto-writing "Proto-writing") and [History of writing](/wiki/History_of_writing "History of writing")

Chinese characters are accepted as representing one of four independent inventions of writing in human history.[[b]](#cite_note-4) In each instance, writing evolved from a system using two distinct types of [ideographs](/wiki/Ideograph "Ideograph")—either pictographs visually depicting objects or concepts, or fixed [signs](/wiki/Sign_(semiotics) "Sign (semiotics)") representing concepts only by shared convention. These systems are classified as [proto-writing](/wiki/Proto-writing "Proto-writing"), because the techniques they used were insufficient to carry the meaning of spoken language by themselves.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu20002-5)

Various innovations were required for Chinese characters to emerge from proto-writing. Firstly, pictographs became distinct from simple pictures in use and appearance—for example, the pictograph 大, meaning 'large', was originally a picture of a large man, but one would need to be aware of its specific meaning in order to interpret the sequence 大鹿 as signifying 'large deer', rather than being a picture of a large man and a deer next to one another. Due to this process of abstraction, as well as to make characters easier to write, pictographs gradually became more simplified and regularized—often to the extent that the original objects represented are no longer obvious.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu20003–4-6)

This proto-writing system was limited to representing a relatively narrow range of ideas with a comparatively small library of symbols. This compelled innovations that allowed for symbols which indicated elements of spoken language directly.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu20005-7) In each historical case, this was accomplished by some form of the [rebus](/wiki/Rebus "Rebus") technique, where the symbol for a word is used to indicate a different word with a similar pronunciation, depending on context.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198859Li202048-8) This allowed for words that lacked a plausible pictographic representation to be written down for the first time. This technique preempted more sophisticated methods of character creation that would further expand the lexicon. The process whereby writing emerged from proto-writing took place over a long period; when the purely pictorial use of symbols disappeared, leaving only those representing spoken words, the process was complete.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200011,_16-9)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

Main article: [Chinese character classification](/wiki/Chinese_character_classification "Chinese character classification")

Chinese characters have been used in several different [writing systems](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") throughout history. A writing system is most commonly defined to include the written symbols themselves, called *[graphemes](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme")*—which may include characters, numerals, or punctuation—as well as the rules by which they are used to record language.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu20001Handel20194–5-10) Chinese characters are [logographs](/wiki/Logograph "Logograph"), which are graphemes that represent units of meaning in a language. Specifically, characters represent a language's [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme"), its most basic units of meaning. Morphemes in Chinese—and therefore the characters used to write them—are nearly always a single syllable in length. In some special cases, characters may denote non-morphemic syllables as well; due to this, [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") is often characterized as [morphosyllabic](/wiki/Morphosyllabic "Morphosyllabic").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200022–26Norman198874-11)[[c]](#cite_note-13) Logographs may be contrasted with [letters](/wiki/Letter_(alphabet) "Letter (alphabet)") in an [alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet"), which generally represent [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"), the distinct units of sound used by speakers of a language.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200013–15Coulmas1991104–109-14) Despite their origins in picture-writing, Chinese characters are no longer ideographs capable of representing ideas directly; their comprehension relies on the reader's knowledge of the particular language being written.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202056–57Boltz19943–4-15)

The areas where Chinese characters were historically used—sometimes collectively termed the *[Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere")*—have a long tradition of [lexicography](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography") attempting to explain and refine their use; for most of history, analysis revolved around a model first popularized in the 2nd-century **[Shuowen Jiezi](/wiki/Shuowen_Jiezi "Shuowen Jiezi")** dictionary.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201910,_51YongPeng200895–98-16) More recent models have analysed the methods used to create characters, how characters are structured, and how they function in a given writing system.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200019,_162–168-17)

### Structural analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Structural analysis")]

Most characters can be analysed structurally as compounds made of smaller [components](/wiki/Chinese_character_components "Chinese character components") (部件; *bùjiàn*), which are often independent characters in their own right, adjusted to occupy a given position in the compound.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz201157,_60-18) Components within a character may serve a specific function—phonetic components provide a hint for the character's pronunciation, and semantic components indicate some element of the character's meaning. Components that serve neither function may be classified as pure signs with no particular meaning, other than their presence distinguishing one character from another.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200014–18-19)

A straightforward structural classification scheme may consist of three pure classes of semantographs, phonographs, and signs—having only semantic, phonetic, and form components respectively—as well as classes corresponding to each combination of component types.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYin200797–100Su2014102–111-20) Of the 3500 characters that are frequently used in Standard Chinese, pure semantographs are estimated to be the rarest, accounting for about 5% of the lexicon, followed by pure signs with 18%, and semantic–form and phonetic–form compounds together accounting for 19%. The remaining 58% are phono-semantic compounds.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYang2008147–148-21)

The 20th-century Chinese palaeographer [Qiu Xigui](/wiki/Qiu_Xigui "Qiu Xigui") presented three principles of character function adapted from earlier proposals by [Tang Lan](/w/index.php?title=Tang_Lan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tang Lan (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%94%90%E8%98%AD "zh:唐蘭")] and [Chen Mengjia](/wiki/Chen_Mengjia "Chen Mengjia"),[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemattè202214-22) with *semantographs* describing all characters with forms wholly related to their meaning, regardless of the method by which the meaning was originally depicted; *phonographs* that include a phonetic component; and *loangraphs* encompassing existing characters that have been borrowed to write other words. Qiu also acknowledged the existence of character classes that fall outside of these principles, such as pure signs.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000163–171-23)

### Semantographs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Semantographs")]

#### Pictographs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pictographs")]

Graphical evolution of pictographs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Evo-r%C3%AC.svg/330px-Evo-r%C3%AC.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Evo-r%C3%AC.svg)

日 ('Sun')

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Evo-sh%C4%81n.svg/330px-Evo-sh%C4%81n.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Evo-sh%C4%81n.svg)

山 ('mountain')

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Evo-xi%C3%A0ng.svg/330px-Evo-xi%C3%A0ng.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Evo-xi%C3%A0ng.svg)

象 ('elephant')

Most of the oldest characters are [pictographs](/wiki/Pictograph "Pictograph") (象形; *xiàngxíng*), representational pictures of physical objects.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng200819-24) Examples include 日 ('Sun'), 月 ('Moon'), and 木 ('tree'). Over time, the forms of pictographs have been simplified in order to make them easier to write.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200044–45Zhou200361-25) As a result, modern readers generally cannot deduce what many pictographs were originally meant to resemble; without knowing the context of their origin in picture-writing, they may be interpreted instead as pure signs. However, if a pictograph's use in compounds still reflects its original meaning, as with 日 in 晴 ('clear sky'), it can still be analysed as a semantic component.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200018–19-26)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000154Norman198868-27)

Pictographs have often been extended from their original meanings to take on additional layers of metaphor and [synecdoche](/wiki/Synecdoche "Synecdoche"), which sometimes displace the character's original sense. When this process results in excessive ambiguity between distinct senses written with the same character, it is usually resolved by new compounds being derived to represent particular senses.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYip200039–42-28)

#### Indicatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Indicatives")]

Indicatives (指事; *zhǐshì*), also called *simple ideographs* or *self-explanatory characters*,[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng200819-24) are visual representations of abstract concepts that lack any tangible form. Examples include 上 ('up') and 下 ('down')—these characters were originally written as dots placed above and below a line, and later evolved into their present forms with less potential for graphical ambiguity in context.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200046-29) More complex indicatives include 凸 ('convex'), 凹 ('concave'), and 平 ('flat and level').[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198868Qiu2000185–187-30)

#### Compound ideographs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Compound ideographs")]

The compound character 好 illustrated as its component characters 女 and 子 positioned side by side

Compound ideographs (会意; 會意; *huìyì*)—also called *logical aggregates*, *associative idea characters*, or *syssemantographs*—combine other characters to convey a new, synthetic meaning. A canonical example is 明 ('bright'), interpreted as the juxtaposition of the two brightest objects in the sky: 日 ('Sun') and 月 ('Moon'), together expressing their shared quality of brightness. Other examples include 休 ('rest'), composed of pictographs 人 ('man') and 木 ('tree'), and 好 ('good'), composed of 女 ('woman') and 子 ('child').[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200015,_190–202-31)

Many traditional examples of compound ideographs are now believed to have actually originated as phono-semantic compounds, made obscure by subsequent changes in pronunciation.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESampsonChen2013261-32) For example, the **Shuowen Jiezi** describes 信 ('trust') as an ideographic compound of 人 ('man') and 言 ('speech'), but modern analyses instead identify it as a phono-semantic compound—though with disagreement as to which component is phonetic.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000155-33) [Peter A. Boodberg](/wiki/Peter_A._Boodberg "Peter A. Boodberg") and [William G. Boltz](/wiki/William_G._Boltz "William G. Boltz") go so far as to deny that any compound ideographs were devised in antiquity, maintaining that secondary readings that are now lost are responsible for the apparent absence of phonetic indicators,[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz1994104–110-34) but their arguments have been rejected by other scholars.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESampsonChen2013265–268-35)

### Phonographs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Phonographs")]

#### Phono-semantic compounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Phono-semantic compounds")]

Phono-semantic compounds (形声; 形聲; *xíngshēng*) are composed of at least one semantic component and one phonetic component.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198868-36) They may be formed by one of several methods, often by adding a phonetic component to disambiguate a loangraph, or by adding a semantic component to represent a specific extension of a character's meaning.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000154-37) Examples of phono-semantic compounds include 河 (*hé*; 'river'), 湖 (*hú*; 'lake'), 流 (*liú*; 'stream'), 沖 (*chōng*; 'surge'), and 滑 (*huá*; 'slippery'). Each of these characters have three short strokes on their left-hand side: 氵, a simplified combining form of ⽔ ('water'). This component serves a semantic function in each example, indicating the character has some meaning related to water. The remainder of each character is its phonetic component: 湖 (*hú*) is pronounced identically to 胡 (*hú*) in Standard Chinese, 河 (*hé*) is pronounced similarly to 可 (*kě*), and 沖 (*chōng*) is pronounced similarly to 中 (*zhōng*).[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECruttenden2021167–168-38)

The phonetic components of most compounds may only provide an approximate pronunciation, even before subsequent sound shifts in the spoken language. Some characters may only have the same initial or final sound of a syllable in common with phonetic components.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams2010-39) A [phonetic series](/wiki/Phonetic_series_(Chinese_characters) "Phonetic series (Chinese characters)") comprises all the characters created using the same phonetic component, which may have diverged significantly in their pronunciations over time. For example, 茶 (*chá*; *caa4*; 'tea') and 途 (*tú*; *tou4*; 'route') are characters in the phonetic series using 余 (*yú*; *jyu4*), a literary first-person pronoun. Their [Old Chinese](/wiki/Old_Chinese "Old Chinese") pronunciations were similar, but the phonetic component no longer serves as a useful hint for their pronunciation in modern [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") due to subsequent sound shifts—demonstrated here in both their [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") readings.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogelsang202151–52-40)

### Loangraphs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Loangraphs")]

The phenomenon of existing characters being adapted to write other words with similar pronunciations was necessary in the initial development of Chinese writing, and has remained common throughout its subsequent history. Some loangraphs (假借; *jiǎjiè*; 'borrowing') are introduced to represent words previously lacking a written form—this is often the case with abstract grammatical particles such as [之](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B9%8B#Chinese "wikt:之") and [其](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%B6#Chinese "wikt:其").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000261–265-41) The process of characters being borrowed as loangraphs should not be conflated with the distinct process of semantic extension, where a word acquires additional senses, which often remain written with the same character. As both processes often result in a single character form being used to write several distinct meanings, loangraphs are often misidentified as being the result of semantic extension, and vice versa.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000273–274,_302-42)

Loangraphs are also used to write words borrowed from other languages, such as the Buddhist terminology introduced to China in antiquity, as well as contemporary non-Chinese words and names. For example, each character in the name 加拿大 (*Jiānádà*; 'Canada') is often used as a loangraph for its respective syllable. However, the barrier between a character's pronunciation and meaning is never total; when transcribing into Chinese, loangraphs are often chosen deliberately as to create certain connotations. This is regularly done with corporate brand names—for example, [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola")'s Chinese name is 可口可乐; 可口可樂 (*Kěkǒu Kělè*; 'delicious enjoyable').[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor201430–32-43)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey198760-44)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGnanadesikan201161-45)

### Signs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Signs")]

Some characters and components are pure [signs](/wiki/Sign_(semiotics) "Sign (semiotics)"), with meanings merely stemming from their having a fixed and distinct form. Basic examples of pure signs are found with [the numerals](/wiki/Chinese_numerals "Chinese numerals") beyond four, e.g. 五 ('five') and 八 ('eight'), whose forms do not give visual hints to the quantities they represent.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000168Norman198860-46)

### Traditional *Shuowen Jiezi* classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Traditional Shuowen Jiezi classification")]

The **[Shuowen Jiezi](/wiki/Shuowen_Jiezi "Shuowen Jiezi")** is a character dictionary authored c. 100 CE by the scholar [Xu Shen](/wiki/Xu_Shen "Xu Shen"). In its postface, Xu analyses what he sees as all the methods by which characters are created. Later authors iterated upon Xu's analysis, developing a categorization scheme known as the 'six writings' (六书; 六書; *liùshū*), which identifies every character with one of six categories that had previously been mentioned in the **Shuowen Jiezi**. For nearly two millennia, this scheme was the primary framework for character analysis used throughout the Sinosphere.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198867–69Handel201948-47) Xu based most of his analysis on examples of Qin seal script that were written down several centuries before his time—these were usually the oldest specimens available to him, though he stated he was aware of the existence of even older forms.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988170–171-48) The first five categories are pictographs, indicatives, compound ideographs, phono-semantic compounds, and loangraphs. The sixth category is given by Xu as 轉注 (*zhuǎnzhù*; 'reversed and refocused'); however, its definition is unclear, and it is generally disregarded by modern scholars.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201948–49-49)

Modern scholars agree that the theory presented in the **Shuowen Jiezi** is problematic, failing to fully capture the nature of Chinese writing, both in the present, as well as at the time Xu was writing.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000153–154,_161Norman1988170-50) Traditional Chinese lexicography as embodied in the **Shuowen Jiezi** has suggested implausible etymologies for some characters.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2013102–108Norman198869-51) Moreover, several categories are considered to be ill-defined—for example, it is unclear whether characters like 大 ('large') should be classified as pictographs or indicatives.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000154-37) However, awareness of the 'six writings' model has remained a common component of character literacy, and often serves as a tool for students memorizing characters.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201943-52)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Chinese script styles](/wiki/Chinese_script_styles "Chinese script styles") and [History of the Chinese language](/wiki/History_of_the_Chinese_language "History of the Chinese language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Comparative_evolution_of_Cuneiform%2C_Egyptian_and_Chinese_characters.svg/250px-Comparative_evolution_of_Cuneiform%2C_Egyptian_and_Chinese_characters.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comparative_evolution_of_Cuneiform,_Egyptian_and_Chinese_characters.svg)

Diagram comparing the abstraction of pictographs in cuneiform, Egyptian hieroglyphs, and Chinese characters – from an 1870 publication by French Egyptologist [Gaston Maspero](/wiki/Gaston_Maspero "Gaston Maspero")[[A]](#cite_note-53)

The broadest trend in the evolution of Chinese characters over their history has been simplification, both in graphical *shape* (字形; *zìxíng*), the "external appearances of individual graphs", and in graphical *form* (字体; 字體; *zìtǐ*), "overall changes in the distinguishing features of graphic[al] shape and calligraphic style, ... in most cases refer[ring] to rather obvious and rather substantial changes".[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200044–45-54) The traditional notion of an orderly procession of script styles, each suddenly appearing and displacing the one previous, has been disproven by later scholarship and archaeological work. Instead, scripts evolved gradually, with several distinct styles often coexisting within a given area.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200059–60,_66-55)

### Traditional invention narrative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Traditional invention narrative")]

Several of the [Chinese classics](/wiki/Chinese_classics "Chinese classics") indicate that [knotted cords](/wiki/Chinese_knotting#Recordkeeping "Chinese knotting") were used to keep records prior to the invention of writing.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemattè202279–80-56) Works that reference the practice include chapter 80 of the **[Tao Te Ching](/wiki/Tao_Te_Ching "Tao Te Ching")**[[B]](#cite_note-57) and the "[Xici](/wiki/Xici "Xici") II" commentary to the **[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")**.[[C]](#cite_note-58) According to one tradition, Chinese characters were invented during the 3rd millennium BCE by [Cangjie](/wiki/Cangjie "Cangjie"), a scribe of the legendary [Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor"). Cangjie is said to have invented symbols called 字 (*zì*) due to his frustration with the limitations of knotting, taking inspiration from his study of the tracks of animals, landscapes, and the stars in the sky. On the day that these first characters were created, grain rained down from the sky; that night, the people heard the wailing of ghosts and demons, lamenting that humans could no longer be cheated.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYangAn200884–86-59)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz1994130–138-60)

### Neolithic precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Neolithic precursors")]

Main article: [Neolithic symbols in China](/wiki/Neolithic_symbols_in_China "Neolithic symbols in China")

Collections of graphs and pictures have been discovered at the sites of several [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") settlements throughout the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") valley, including [Jiahu](/wiki/Jiahu "Jiahu") (c. 6500 BCE), [Dadiwan](/wiki/Dadiwan "Dadiwan") and [Damaidi](/wiki/Damaidi "Damaidi") (6th millennium BCE), and [Banpo](/wiki/Banpo "Banpo") (5th millennium BCE). Symbols at each site were inscribed or drawn onto artefacts, appearing one at a time and without indicating any greater context. Qiu concluded, "We simply possess no basis for saying that they were already being used to record language."[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200031-61) A historical connection with the symbols used by the late Neolithic [Dawenkou culture](/wiki/Dawenkou_culture "Dawenkou culture") (c. 4300 – c. 2600 BCE) in Shandong has been deemed possible by palaeographers, with Qiu concluding that they "cannot be definitively treated as primitive writing, nevertheless they are symbols which resemble most the ancient pictographic script discovered thus far in China... They undoubtedly can be viewed as the forerunners of primitive writing."[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200039-62)

### Oracle bone script

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Oracle bone script")]

Main article: [Oracle bone script](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script")

Oracle bone script

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/%E5%A4%A9-oracle.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-oracle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-oracle.svg)

'Heaven'

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/%E9%A6%AC-oracle.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-oracle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-oracle.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/%E6%97%85-oracle.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-oracle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-oracle.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/%E6%AD%A3-oracle.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-oracle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-oracle.svg)

'straight'

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/%E9%9F%8B-oracle.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-oracle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-oracle.svg)

'leather'

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Shang_dynasty_inscribed_scapula.jpg/330px-Shang_dynasty_inscribed_scapula.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shang_dynasty_inscribed_scapula.jpg)

Ox scapula inscribed with characters recording the result of divinations – dated c. 1200 BCE

The oldest attested Chinese writing comprises a body of inscriptions produced during the [Late Shang](/wiki/Late_Shang "Late Shang") period (c. 1250 – 1050 BCE), with the very earliest examples from the reign of [Wu Ding](/wiki/Wu_Ding "Wu Ding") dated between 1250 and 1200 BCE.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz199974,_107–108Liu_et_al.2017155–175-63) Many of these inscriptions were made on [oracle bones](/wiki/Oracle_bone "Oracle bone")—usually either ox [scapulae](/wiki/Scapula "Scapula") or turtle [plastrons](/wiki/Turtle_shell#Plastron "Turtle shell")—and recorded official [divinations](/wiki/Divination "Divination") carried out by the Shang royal house. Contemporaneous inscriptions in a related but distinct style were also made on ritual bronze vessels. This [oracle bone script](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script") (甲骨文; *jiǎgǔwén*) was first documented in 1899, after specimens were discovered being sold as "dragon bones" for medicinal purposes, with the symbols carved into them identified as early character forms. By 1928, the source of the bones had been traced to a village near [Anyang](/wiki/Anyang "Anyang") in [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan")—discovered to be the site of [Yin](/wiki/Yinxu "Yinxu"), the final Shang capital—which was excavated by a team led by [Li Ji](/wiki/Li_Ji_(archaeologist) "Li Ji (archaeologist)") from the [Academia Sinica](/wiki/Academia_Sinica "Academia Sinica") between 1928 and 1937.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiuChen20126-64) To date, over 150000 oracle bone fragments have been found.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKern20101Wilkinson2012681–682-65)

Oracle bone inscriptions recorded divinations undertaken to communicate with the spirits of royal ancestors. The inscriptions range from a few characters in length at their shortest, to several dozen at their longest. The Shang king would communicate with his ancestors by means of [scapulimancy](/wiki/Scapulimancy "Scapulimancy"), inquiring about subjects such as the royal family, military success, and the weather. Inscriptions were made in the divination material itself before and after it had been cracked by exposure to heat; they generally include a record of the questions posed, as well as the answers as interpreted in the cracks.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeightley197828–42-66)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKern20101-67) A minority of bones feature characters that were inked with a brush before their strokes were incised; the evidence of this also shows that the conventional [stroke orders](/wiki/Stroke_order "Stroke order") used by later calligraphers had already been established for many characters by this point.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeightley197846–47-68)

Oracle bone script is the direct ancestor of later forms of written Chinese. The oldest known inscriptions already represent a well-developed writing system, which suggests an initial emergence predating the late 2nd millennium BCE. Although written Chinese is first attested in official divinations, it is widely believed that writing was also used for other purposes during the Shang, but that the media used in other contexts—likely [bamboo and wooden slips](/wiki/Bamboo_and_wooden_slips "Bamboo and wooden slips")—were less durable than bronzes or oracle bones, and have not been preserved.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz1986424Kern20102-69)

### Zhou scripts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Zhou scripts")]

See also: [Chinese bronze inscriptions](/wiki/Chinese_bronze_inscriptions "Chinese bronze inscriptions"), [Bamboo and wooden slips](/wiki/Bamboo_and_wooden_slips "Bamboo and wooden slips"), and [Seal script](/wiki/Seal_script "Seal script")

Bronze script

[![天](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/%E5%A4%A9-bronze.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-bronze.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-bronze.svg)

[![馬](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/%E9%A6%AC-bronze.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-bronze.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-bronze.svg)

[![旅](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/%E6%97%85-bronze.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-bronze.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-bronze.svg)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/%E6%AD%A3-bronze.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-bronze.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-bronze.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/%E9%9F%8B-bronze.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-bronze.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-bronze.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg/330px-Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg)

The [Shi Qiang *pan*](/wiki/Shi_Qiang_pan "Shi Qiang pan"), a bronze ritual basin bearing inscriptions describing the deeds and virtues of the first seven Zhou kings – dated c. 900 BCE[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaughnessy19911–4-70)

As early as the Shang, the oracle bone script existed as a simplified form alongside another that was used in bamboo books, in addition to elaborate pictorial forms often used in clan emblems. These other forms have been preserved in [bronze script](/wiki/Bronze_script "Bronze script") (金文; *jīnwén*), where inscriptions were made using a stylus in a clay mould, which was then used to cast [ritual bronzes](/wiki/Chinese_ritual_bronzes "Chinese ritual bronzes").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200063–66-71) These differences in technique generally resulted in character forms that were less angular in appearance than their oracle bone script counterparts.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200088–89-72)

Study of these bronze inscriptions has revealed that the mainstream script underwent slow, gradual evolution during the late Shang, which continued during the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (c. 1046 – 256 BCE) until assuming the form now known as *[small seal script](/wiki/Small_seal_script "Small seal script")* (小篆; *xiǎozhuàn*) within the Zhou [state of Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200076–78-73)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen2003-74) Other scripts in use during the late Zhou include the [bird-worm seal script](/wiki/Bird-worm_seal_script "Bird-worm seal script") (鸟虫书; 鳥蟲書; *niǎochóngshū*), as well as the regional forms used in non-Qin states. Examples of these styles were preserved as variants in the **Shuowen Jiezi**.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis2003-75) Historically, Zhou forms were collectively known as *[large seal script](/wiki/Large_seal_script "Large seal script")* (大篆; *dàzhuàn*), though Qiu refrained from using this term due to its lack of precision.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200077-76)

### Qin unification and small seal script

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Qin unification and small seal script")]

Main article: [Small seal script](/wiki/Small_seal_script "Small seal script")

Small seal script

[![天](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/%E5%A4%A9-seal.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-seal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-seal.svg)

[![馬](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/%E9%A6%AC-seal.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-seal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-seal.svg)

[![旅](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/%E6%97%85-seal.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-seal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-seal.svg)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/%E6%AD%A3-seal.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-seal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-seal.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/%E9%9F%8B-seal.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-seal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-seal.svg)

Following [Qin's conquest](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification") of the other Chinese states that culminated in the founding of the imperial [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") in 221 BCE, the Qin small seal script was standardized for use throughout the entire country under the direction of Chancellor [Li Si](/wiki/Li_Si "Li Si").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltz1994156-77) It was traditionally believed that Qin scribes only used small seal script, and the later clerical script was a sudden invention during the early Han. However, more than one script was used by Qin scribes—a rectilinear vulgar style had also been in use in Qin for centuries prior to the wars of unification. The popularity of this form grew as writing became more widespread.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000104–107-78)

### Clerical script

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Clerical script")]

Main article: [Clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script")

Clerical script

[![天](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/%E5%A4%A9-clerical-han.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-clerical-han.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-clerical-han.svg)

[![馬](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/%E9%A6%AC-clerical-han.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-clerical-han.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-clerical-han.svg)

[![旅](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/%E6%97%85-clerical-han.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-clerical-han.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-clerical-han.svg)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/%E6%AD%A3-clerical-han.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-clerical-han.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-clerical-han.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/%E9%9F%8B-clerical-han.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-clerical-han.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-clerical-han.svg)

By the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") (c. 475 – 221 BCE), an immature form of [clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script") (隶书; 隸書; *lìshū*) had emerged based on the vulgar form developed within Qin, often called "early clerical" or "proto-clerical".[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu200059,_119-79) The proto-clerical script evolved gradually; by the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BCE – 220 CE), it had arrived at a mature form, also called 八分 (*bāfēn*). Bamboo slips discovered during the late 20th century point to this maturation being completed during the reign of [Emperor Wu of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") (r. 141–87 BCE). This process, called *[libian](/wiki/Libian "Libian")* (隶变; 隸變), involved character forms being mutated and simplified, with many components being consolidated, substituted, or omitted. In turn, the components themselves were regularized to use fewer, straighter, and more well-defined strokes. As a result, clerical script largely lacks the pictorial qualities still evident in seal script.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000119–124-80)

Around the midpoint of the [Eastern Han](/wiki/Eastern_Han "Eastern Han") (25–220 CE), a simplified and easier form of clerical script appeared, which Qiu termed 'neo-clerical' (新隶体; 新隸體; *xīnlìtǐ*).[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000113,_139,_466-81) By the end of the Han, this had become the dominant script used by scribes, though clerical script remained in use for formal works, such as engraved [stelae](/wiki/Stelae "Stelae"). Qiu described neo-clerical as a transitional form between clerical and [regular script](/wiki/Regular_script "Regular script") which remained in use through the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period (220–280 CE) and beyond.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000138–139-82)

### Cursive and semi-cursive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Cursive and semi-cursive")]

Cursive script

[![天](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/%E5%A4%A9-caoshu.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-caoshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-caoshu.svg)

[![馬](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/%E9%A6%AC-caoshu.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-caoshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-caoshu.svg)

[![旅](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/%E6%97%85-caoshu.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-caoshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-caoshu.svg)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/%E6%AD%A3-caoshu.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-caoshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-caoshu.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/%E9%9F%8B-caoshu.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-caoshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-caoshu.svg)

[Cursive script](/wiki/Cursive_script_(East_Asia) "Cursive script (East Asia)") (草书; 草書; *cǎoshū*) was in use as early as 24 BCE, synthesizing elements of the vulgar writing that had originated in Qin with flowing cursive brushwork. By the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)") (266–420), the Han cursive style became known as 章草 (*zhāngcǎo*; 'orderly cursive'), sometimes known in English as 'clerical cursive', 'ancient cursive', or 'draft cursive'. Some attribute this name to the fact that the style was considered more orderly than a later form referred to as 今草 (*jīncǎo*; 'modern cursive'), which had first emerged during the Jin and was influenced by semi-cursive and regular script. This later form was exemplified by the work of figures like [Wang Xizhi](/wiki/Wang_Xizhi "Wang Xizhi") (fl. 4th century), who is often regarded as the most important calligrapher in Chinese history.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000130–148-83)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnechtgesChang20141257–1259-84)

Semi-cursive script

[![天](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/%E5%A4%A9-xingshu.svg/60px-%E5%A4%A9-xingshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%A4%A9-xingshu.svg)

[![馬](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/%E9%A6%AC-xingshu.svg/60px-%E9%A6%AC-xingshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%A6%AC-xingshu.svg)

[![旅](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/%E6%97%85-xingshu.svg/60px-%E6%97%85-xingshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%97%85-xingshu.svg)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/%E6%AD%A3-xingshu.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-xingshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-xingshu.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/%E9%9F%8B-xingshu.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-xingshu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-xingshu.svg)

An early form of [semi-cursive script](/wiki/Semi-cursive_script "Semi-cursive script") (行书; 行書; *xíngshū*; 'running script') can be identified during the late Han, with its development stemming from a cursive form of neo-clerical script. Liu Desheng (刘德升; 劉德升; fl. 2nd century CE) is traditionally recognized as the inventor of the semi-cursive style, though accreditations of this kind often indicate a given style's early masters, rather than its earliest practitioners. Later analysis has suggested popular origins for semi-cursive, as opposed to it being an invention of Liu.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000113,_139–142-85) It can be characterized partly as the result of clerical forms being written more quickly, without formal rules of technique or composition—what would be discrete strokes in clerical script frequently flow together instead. The semi-cursive style is commonly adopted in contemporary handwriting.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202051Qiu2000149Norman198870-86)

[![正](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/%E6%AD%A3-kaishu.svg/60px-%E6%AD%A3-kaishu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%AD%A3-kaishu.svg)

[![韋](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/%E9%9F%8B-kaishu.svg/60px-%E9%9F%8B-kaishu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E9%9F%8B-kaishu.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/%E5%A7%93%E8%A7%A3_Digidepo_1287529_00000014%282%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-%E5%A7%93%E8%A7%A3_Digidepo_1287529_00000014%282%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%A7%93%E8%A7%A3_Digidepo_1287529_00000014(2)_(cropped).jpg)

Page from a Song-era publication printed using a regular script style[[D]](#cite_note-87)

[Regular script](/wiki/Regular_script "Regular script") (楷书; 楷書; *kǎishū*), based on clerical and semi-cursive forms, is the predominant form in which characters are written and printed.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000113,_149-88) Its innovations have traditionally been credited to the calligrapher [Zhong Yao](/wiki/Zhong_Yao "Zhong Yao"), who lived in the state of [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei") (extant 220–266); he is often called the "father of regular script".[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChan2020125-89) The earliest surviving writing in regular script comprises copies of Zhong Yao's work, including at least one copy by Wang Xizhi. Characteristics of regular script include the 'pause' (頓; *dùn*) technique used to end horizontal strokes, as well as heavy tails on diagonal strokes made going down and to the right. It developed further during the [Eastern Jin](/wiki/Eastern_Jin "Eastern Jin") (317–420) in the hands of Wang Xizhi and his son [Wang Xianzhi](/wiki/Wang_Xianzhi_(calligrapher) "Wang Xianzhi (calligrapher)").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000143-90) However, most Jin-era writers continued to use neo-clerical and semi-cursive styles in their daily writing. It was not until the [Northern and Southern period](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_period "Northern and Southern period") (420–589) that regular script became the predominant form.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000144–145-91) The system of [imperial examinations](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination") for the civil service established during the [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") (581–618) required test takers to write in [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese") using regular script, which contributed to the prevalence of both throughout later Chinese history.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202041-92)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Structure")]

Each character of a text is written within a uniform square allotted for it. As part of the evolution from seal script into clerical script, character components became regularized as discrete series of [strokes](/wiki/Chinese_character_strokes "Chinese character strokes") (笔画; 筆畫; *bǐhuà*).[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202054,_196–197Peking_University2004148–152Zhou200388-93) Strokes can be considered both the basic unit of handwriting, as well as the writing system's basic unit of [graphemic](/wiki/Graphemic "Graphemic") organization. In clerical and regular script, individual strokes traditionally belong to one of eight categories according to their technique and graphemic function. In what is known as the *[Eight Principles of Yong](/wiki/Eight_Principles_of_Yong "Eight Principles of Yong")*, calligraphers practise their technique using the character 永 (*yǒng*; 'eternity'), which can be written with one stroke of each type.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198886Zhou200358Zhang2013-94) In ordinary writing, 永 is now written with five strokes instead of eight, and a system of five basic stroke types is commonly employed in analysis—with certain compound strokes treated as sequences of basic strokes made in a single motion.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi200965–66Zhou200388-95)

Characters are constructed according to predictable visual patterns. Some components have distinct combining forms when occupying specific positions within a character—for example, the ⼑ ('knife') component appears as 刂 on the right side of characters, but as ⺈ at the top of characters.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201943–44-96) The order in which components are drawn within a character is fixed. The [order in which the strokes of a component are drawn](/wiki/Stroke_order "Stroke order") is also largely fixed, but may vary according to several different standards.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYin201658–59-97)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers2019106–116-98) This is summed up in practice with a few rules of thumb, including that characters are generally assembled from left to right, then from top to bottom, with "enclosing" components started before, then closed after, the components they enclose.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi200970-99) For example, 永 is drawn in the following order:

Sequence and placement of the strokes in 永

| Character | Stroke | |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | 1 | [㇔](/wiki/File:CJK_Stroke_D_(1).svg) |
| 2 | [㇚](/wiki/File:CJK_Stroke_SG.svg) |
| 3 | [乛](/wiki/File:Cjk_m_str_ht.svg) |
| 4 | [丿](/wiki/File:Regular_Style_CJKV_Radical_004_(1).svg) |
| 5 | [㇏](/wiki/File:Cjk_m_str_p.svg) |

[![A sequence showing the results while writing the character 永 as each stroke is added](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/%E6%B0%B8-bw.png/500px-%E6%B0%B8-bw.png)](/wiki/File:%E6%B0%B8-bw.png)

### Variant characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Variant characters")]

Main article: [Variant Chinese characters](/wiki/Variant_Chinese_characters "Variant Chinese characters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Variants_of_Kangxi_radical_213_%27turtle%27.svg/500px-Variants_of_Kangxi_radical_213_%27turtle%27.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Variants_of_Kangxi_radical_213_%27turtle%27.svg)

Variants of the Chinese character for 'turtle', collected c. 1800 from printed sources.[[E]](#cite_note-100) The traditional form 龜 (left) is used in Taiwan and Hong Kong. The simplified form 龟 (not pictured) is used in mainland China, and the simplified form 亀 (also not pictured) is used in Japan.

Over a character's history, [variant character forms](/wiki/Variant_character_form "Variant character form") (异体字; 異體字; *yìtǐzì*) emerge via several processes. Variant forms have distinct structures, but represent the same morpheme; as such, they can be considered instances of the same underlying character. This is comparable to visually distinct double-storey |a| and single-storey |ɑ| forms both representing the Latin letter ⟨[A](/wiki/A "A")⟩. Variants also emerge for aesthetic reasons, to make handwriting easier, or to correct what the writer perceives to be errors in a character's form.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000204–215,_373-101) Individual components may be replaced with visually, phonetically, or semantically similar alternatives.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200357–60,_63–65-102) The boundary between character structure and style—and thus whether forms represent different characters, or are merely variants of the same character—is often non-trivial or unclear.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000297–300,_373-103)

For example, prior to the Qin dynasty the character meaning 'bright' was written as either 明 or 朙—with either 日 ('Sun') or 囧 ('window') on the left, and 月 ('Moon') on the right. As part of the Qin programme to standardize small seal script across China, the 朙 form was promoted. Some scribes ignored this, and continued to write the character as 明. However, the increased usage of 朙 was followed by the proliferation of a third variant: 眀, with 目 ('eye') on the left—likely derived as a contraction of 朙. Ultimately, 明 became the character's standard form.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBökset200616,_19-104)

### Layout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Layout")]

Further information: [Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts](/wiki/Horizontal_and_vertical_writing_in_East_Asian_scripts "Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts")

See also: [Chinese punctuation](/wiki/Chinese_punctuation "Chinese punctuation"), [Japanese punctuation](/wiki/Japanese_punctuation "Japanese punctuation"), and [Korean punctuation](/wiki/Korean_punctuation "Korean punctuation")

From the earliest inscriptions until the 20th century, texts were generally laid out vertically—with characters written from top to bottom in columns, arranged from right to left. [Word boundaries](/wiki/Word#Word_boundaries "Word") are generally not indicated with [spaces](/wiki/Space_(punctuation) "Space (punctuation)"). A horizontal writing direction—with characters written from left to right in rows, arranged from top to bottom—only became predominant in the Sinosphere during the 20th century as a result of Western influence.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202054Handel201927Keightley197850-105) Many publications outside mainland China continue to use the traditional vertical writing direction.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014372–373Bachner2014245-106) Western influence also resulted in the generalized use of punctuation being widely adopted in print during the 19th and 20th centuries. Prior to this, the context of a passage was considered adequate to guide readers; this was enabled by characters being easier to read than alphabets when [written without spaces or punctuation](/wiki/Scriptio_continua "Scriptio continua") due to their more discretized shapes.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamHarbsmeier1998175–176TaylorTaylor2014374–375-107)

## Methods of writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Methods of writing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/%E5%99%B9%E5%99%B9%E8%8C%B6%E9%A4%90%E5%BB%B32021%E5%B9%B47%E6%9C%88%E5%88%9D%E7%9A%84%E5%8D%88%E9%A4%90%E9%A4%90%E7%89%8C-tweaked.jpg/330px-%E5%99%B9%E5%99%B9%E8%8C%B6%E9%A4%90%E5%BB%B32021%E5%B9%B47%E6%9C%88%E5%88%9D%E7%9A%84%E5%8D%88%E9%A4%90%E9%A4%90%E7%89%8C-tweaked.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%99%B9%E5%99%B9%E8%8C%B6%E9%A4%90%E5%BB%B32021%E5%B9%B47%E6%9C%88%E5%88%9D%E7%9A%84%E5%8D%88%E9%A4%90%E9%A4%90%E7%89%8C-tweaked.jpg)

Ordinary handwriting on a lunch menu in Hong Kong. Here, 反 (*fǎn*) is being used as an unofficial short form of 飯 (*fàn*; 'meal') by omitting the latter's ⾷ ('eat') component.

The earliest attested Chinese characters were carved into bone, or marked using a stylus in clay moulds used to cast [ritual bronzes](/wiki/Chinese_ritual_bronzes "Chinese ritual bronzes"). Characters have also been incised into stone, or written in ink onto slips of silk, wood, and bamboo. The [invention of paper](/wiki/History_of_paper "History of paper") for use as a writing medium occurred during the 1st century CE, and is traditionally credited to [Cai Lun](/wiki/Cai_Lun "Cai Lun").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien200123–25,_38–41-108) There are numerous styles, or *scripts* (书; 書; *shū*) in which characters can be written, including the historical forms like seal script and clerical script. Most styles used throughout the Sinosphere originated within China, though they may display regional variation. Styles that have been created outside of China tend to remain localized in their use—these include the Japanese *[edomoji](/wiki/Edomoji "Edomoji")* and Vietnamese *[lệnh thư](/wiki/L%E1%BB%87nh_th%C6%B0 "Lệnh thư")* scripts.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawar2020-109)

### Calligraphy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Calligraphy")]

Main article: [Chinese calligraphy](/wiki/Chinese_calligraphy "Chinese calligraphy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/This_Letter_written_by_Mi_Fei.jpg/250px-This_Letter_written_by_Mi_Fei.jpg)](/wiki/File:This_Letter_written_by_Mi_Fei.jpg)

Chinese calligraphy of mixed styles by the Song-era poet [Mi Fu](/wiki/Mi_Fu "Mi Fu")

Calligraphy was traditionally one of the [four arts](/wiki/Four_arts "Four arts") to be mastered by Chinese scholars, considered to be an artful means of expressing thoughts and teachings. Chinese calligraphy typically makes use of an [ink brush](/wiki/Ink_brush "Ink brush") to write characters. Strict regularity is not required, and character forms may be accentuated to evoke a variety of aesthetic effects.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2009180–183-110) Traditional ideals of calligraphic beauty often tie into broader philosophical concepts native to East Asia. For example, aesthetics can be conceptualized using the framework of [yin and yang](/wiki/Yin_and_yang "Yin and yang"), where the extremes of any number of mutually reinforcing dualities are balanced by the calligrapher—such as the duality between strokes made quickly or slowly, between applying ink heavily or lightly, between characters written with symmetrical or asymmetrical forms, and between characters representing concrete or abstract concepts.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2009175–179-111)

### Printing and typefaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Printing and typefaces")]

Further information: [History of printing in East Asia](/wiki/History_of_printing_in_East_Asia "History of printing in East Asia") and [East Asian typography](/wiki/East_Asian_typography "East Asian typography")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/%E7%9B%A3%E7%8D%84%E9%AB%94%E6%A8%A3%E6%9C%AC.svg/250px-%E7%9B%A3%E7%8D%84%E9%AB%94%E6%A8%A3%E6%9C%AC.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E7%9B%A3%E7%8D%84%E9%AB%94%E6%A8%A3%E6%9C%AC.svg)

Sample of [Prison Gothic](/wiki/Prison_Gothic "Prison Gothic"), a sans-serif typeface

[Woodblock printing](/wiki/Woodblock_printing "Woodblock printing") was invented in China between the 6th and 9th centuries,[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien2001146–147,_159-112) followed by the invention of [moveable type](/wiki/Moveable_type "Moveable type") by [Bi Sheng](/wiki/Bi_Sheng "Bi Sheng") during the 11th century.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedhamTsien2001201–205-113) The increasing use of print during the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") (1368–1644) and [Qing](/wiki/Qing "Qing") dynasties (1644–1912) led to considerable standardization in character forms, which prefigured later script reforms during the 20th century. This print [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography"), exemplified by the 1716 *[Kangxi Dictionary](/wiki/Kangxi_Dictionary "Kangxi Dictionary")*, was later dubbed the **[jiu zixing](/wiki/Jiu_zixing "Jiu zixing")** ('old character shapes').[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng2008280–282,_293–297-114)
Printed Chinese characters may use different [typefaces](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface"),[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi201362-115) of which there are four broad classes in use:[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELunde200823–25-116)

* [Song](/wiki/Ming_typefaces "Ming typefaces") (宋体; 宋體) or Ming (明体; 明體) typefaces—with "Song" generally used with simplified Chinese typefaces, and "Ming" with others—broadly correspond to Western [serif](/wiki/Serif "Serif") styles. Song typefaces are broadly within the tradition of historical Chinese print; both names for the style refer to eras regarded as high points for printing in the Sinosphere. While type during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (960–1279) generally resembled the regular script style of a particular calligrapher, most modern Song typefaces are intended for general purpose use and emphasize neutrality in their design.
* [Sans-serif typefaces](/wiki/East_Asian_Gothic_typeface "East Asian Gothic typeface") are called 'black form' (黑体; 黑體; *hēitǐ*) in Chinese and 'Gothic' (ゴシック体) in Japanese. Sans-serif strokes are rendered as simple lines of even thickness.
* "Kai" typefaces (楷体; 楷體) imitate handwritten regular script.
* [Fangsong](/wiki/Fangsong "Fangsong") typefaces (仿宋体; 仿宋體), called "Song" in Japan, correspond to semi-[script styles](/wiki/Script_typeface "Script typeface") in the Western paradigm.

### Use with computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Use with computers")]

Main article: [Chinese character information technology](/wiki/Chinese_character_information_technology "Chinese character information technology")

Before computers became ubiquitous, earlier electro-mechanical communications devices like [telegraphs](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") and [typewriters](/wiki/Typewriter "Typewriter") were originally designed for use with alphabets, often by means of alphabetic [text encodings](/wiki/Text_encoding "Text encoding") like [Morse code](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code") and [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII"). Adapting these technologies for a writing system that uses thousands of distinct characters was non-trivial.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESu2014218-117)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMullaney201725-118)

#### Input methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Input methods")]

Further information: [Chinese input method](/wiki/Chinese_input_method "Chinese input method") and [Japanese input method](/wiki/Japanese_input_method "Japanese input method")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d9/Interface_of_Weasel_Input_Method.png)](/wiki/File:Interface_of_Weasel_Input_Method.png)

Chinese IME displaying candidates based on pinyin spelling

Chinese characters are predominantly input on computers using a standard keyboard. Many input methods (IMEs) are phonetic, where typists enter characters according to schemes like [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") or [bopomofo](/wiki/Bopomofo "Bopomofo") for Mandarin, [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping") for Cantonese, or [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization") for Japanese. For example, 香港 ('Hong Kong') could be input as `xiang1gang3` using pinyin, or as `hoeng1gong2` using Jyutping.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020152–153-119)

Character input methods may also be based on form, using the shape of characters and existing rules of handwriting to assign unique codes to each character, potentially increasing the speed of typing. Popular form-based input methods include [Wubi](/wiki/Wubi_method "Wubi method") on the mainland, and [Cangjie](/wiki/Cangjie_input_method "Cangjie input method")—named after the mythological inventor of writing—in Taiwan and Hong Kong.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020152–153-119) Often, unnecessary parts are omitted from the encoding according to predictable rules. For example, 疆 ('border') is encoded using the Cangjie method as `NGMWM`, which corresponds to the components `弓土一田一`.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhang2016422-120)

Contextual constraints may be used to improve candidate character selection. When ignoring [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), 知道 and 直刀 are both transcribed as `zhidao`; the system may prioritize which candidate appears first based on context.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESu2014222-121)

#### Encoding and interchange

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Encoding and interchange")]

Main article: [Chinese character encoding](/wiki/Chinese_character_encoding "Chinese character encoding")

See also: [Han unification](/wiki/Han_unification "Han unification")

While special text encodings for Chinese characters were introduced prior to its popularization, *[The Unicode Standard](/wiki/The_Unicode_Standard "The Unicode Standard")* is the predominant text encoding worldwide.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELunde2008193-122) According to the philosophy of the [Unicode Consortium](/wiki/Unicode_Consortium "Unicode Consortium"), each distinct graph is assigned a number in the standard, but specifying its appearance or the particular [allograph](/wiki/Allograph "Allograph") used is a choice made by the engine rendering the text.[[F]](#cite_note-123) Unicode's [Basic Multilingual Plane](/wiki/Basic_Multilingual_Plane "Basic Multilingual Plane") (BMP) represents the standard's 216 smallest code points. Of these, 20992 (or 32%) are assigned to [CJK Unified Ideographs](/wiki/CJK_Unified_Ideographs "CJK Unified Ideographs"), a designation comprising characters used in each of the [Chinese family of scripts](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts"). As of version 17.0, published in 2025, Unicode defines a total of 102998 Chinese characters.[[G]](#cite_note-124)

## Vocabulary and adaptation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Vocabulary and adaptation")]

Further information: [Chinese family of scripts](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts") and [Adoption of Chinese literary culture](/wiki/Adoption_of_Chinese_literary_culture "Adoption of Chinese literary culture")

Writing first emerged during the historical stage of the Chinese language known as *[Old Chinese](/wiki/Old_Chinese "Old Chinese")*. Most characters correspond to morphemes that originally functioned as stand-alone Old Chinese words.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198874–75-125) [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") is the form of written Chinese used in the [classic works](/wiki/Chinese_classics "Chinese classics") of Chinese literature from roughly the 5th century BCE until the 2nd century CE.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogelsang2021xvii–xix-126) This form of the language was imitated by later authors, even as it began to diverge from the language they spoke. This later form, referred to as *Literary Chinese*, remained the predominant written language in China until the 20th century. Its use in the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere") was loosely analogous to that of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") in pre-modern Europe.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201934Norman198883-127) While it was not static over time, Literary Chinese retained many properties of spoken Old Chinese. Informed by the local spoken [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular"), texts were read aloud using [literary and colloquial readings](/wiki/Literary_and_colloquial_readings "Literary and colloquial readings") that varied by region.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198841–42-128) Over time, sound mergers created ambiguities in vernacular speech as more words became homophonic. This ambiguity was often reduced through the introduction of multi-syllable [compound words](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)"),[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson201222-129) which comprise much of the vocabulary in modern varieties of Chinese.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETongLiuMcBride-Chang2009203-130)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYip200018-131)

Over time, use of Literary Chinese spread to neighbouring countries, including Vietnam, Korea, and Japan. Alongside other aspects of Chinese culture, local elites adopted writing for record-keeping, histories, and official communications.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201911–12Kornicki201815–16-132) Excepting hypotheses by some linguists of the latter two sharing a common ancestor, Chinese, Vietnamese, Korean, and Japanese each belong to different [language families](/wiki/Language_families "Language families"),[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201928,_69,_126,_169-133) and tend to function differently from one another. Reading systems were devised to enable non-Chinese speakers to interpret Literary Chinese texts in terms of their native language, a phenomenon that has been variously described as either a form of [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), as *reading by gloss*,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKin2021XII-134) or as a process of translation into and out of Chinese. Compared to other traditions that wrote using alphabets or syllabaries, the literary culture that developed in this context was less directly tied to a specific spoken language. This is exemplified by the cross-linguistic phenomenon of [brushtalk](/wiki/Brushtalk "Brushtalk"), where mutual literacy allowed speakers of different languages to engage in face-to-face conversations.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDenecke2014204–216-135)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki201872–73-136)

Following the introduction of Literary Chinese, characters were later adapted to write many non-Chinese languages spoken throughout the Sinosphere. These new writing systems used characters to write both native vocabulary and the numerous [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") each language had borrowed from Chinese, collectively termed *[Sino-Xenic vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Xenic_vocabulary "Sino-Xenic vocabulary")*. Characters may have native readings, Sino-Xenic readings, or both.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019212-137) Comparison of Sino-Xenic vocabulary across the Sinosphere has been useful in the reconstruction of [Middle Chinese phonology](/wiki/Middle_Chinese_phonology "Middle Chinese phonology").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki2018168-138) Literary Chinese was used in Vietnam during the [millennium of Chinese rule](/wiki/Vietnam_under_Chinese_rule "Vietnam under Chinese rule") that began in 111 BCE. By the 15th century, a system that adapted characters to write Vietnamese called *[chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm")* had fully matured.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019124–125,_133-139) The 2nd century BCE is the earliest possible period for the introduction of writing to Korea; the oldest surviving manuscripts in the country date to the early 5th century CE. Also during the 5th century, writing spread from Korea to Japan.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201964–65-140) Characters were being used to write both Korean and Japanese by the 6th century.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki201857-141) By the late 20th century, characters had largely been replaced with alphabets designed to write Vietnamese and Korean. This leaves Japanese as the only major non-Sinitic language typically written using Chinese characters.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas1997136–138-142)

### Literary and vernacular Chinese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Literary and vernacular Chinese")]

See also: [Reconstructions of Old Chinese](/wiki/Reconstructions_of_Old_Chinese "Reconstructions of Old Chinese") and [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese")

[![Line drawings of various ordinary objects such as books, baskets, buildings, and musical instruments are displayed beside their corresponding Chinese characters](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Chineseprimer3.png/250px-Chineseprimer3.png)](/wiki/File:Chineseprimer3.png)

Excerpt from a 1436 primer on Chinese characters[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbrey1996205-143)

Words in Classical Chinese were generally a single character in length.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198858-144) An estimated 25–30% of the vocabulary used in Classical Chinese texts consists of two-character words.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson201222–23-145) Over time, the introduction of multi-syllable vocabulary into vernacular varieties of Chinese was encouraged by [phonetic shifts](/wiki/Phonetic_shift "Phonetic shift") that increased the number of homophones.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198886–87-146) The most common process of Chinese word formation after the Classical period has been to create compounds of existing words. Words have also been created by appending [affixes](/wiki/Affix "Affix") to words, by [reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication"), and by borrowing words from other languages.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988155–156-147) While multi-syllable words are generally written with one character per syllable, abbreviations are occasionally used.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198874-148) For example, 二十 (*èrshí*; 'twenty') may be written as the contracted form 廿.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201934-149)

Sometimes, different morphemes come to be represented by characters with identical shapes. For example, 行 may represent either 'road' (*xíng*) or the extended sense of 'row' (*háng*)—these morphemes are ultimately [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") that diverged in pronunciation but remained written with the same character. However, Qiu reserved the term *homograph* to describe identically shaped characters with different meanings that emerge via processes other than semantic extension. An example homograph is 铊; 鉈, which originally meant 'weight used at a steelyard' (*tuó*). In the 20th century, this character was created again with the meaning '[thallium](/wiki/Thallium "Thallium")' (*tā*). Both of these characters are phono-semantic compounds with ⾦ ('gold') as the semantic component and 它 as the phonetic component, but the words represented by each are not related.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000301–302-150)

There are a number of 'dialect characters' (方言字; *fāngyánzì*) that are not used in standard [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese"), but reflect the vocabulary of other spoken varieties. The most complete example of an orthography based on a variety other than [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") is [Written Cantonese](/wiki/Written_Cantonese "Written Cantonese"). A common Cantonese character is 冇 (*mou5*; 'to not have'), derived by removing two strokes from 有 (*jau5*; 'to have').[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201959-151) It is common to use standard characters to transcribe previously unwritten words in Chinese dialects when obvious cognates exist. When no obvious cognate exists due to factors like irregular sound changes, semantic drift, or an origin in a non-Chinese language, characters are often borrowed or invented to transcribe the word—either ad hoc, or according to existing principles.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECheungBauer200212–20-152) These new characters are generally phono-semantic compounds.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198875–77-153)
