In Japanese, Chinese characters are referred to as *[kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji")*. During the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period") (710–794), readers and writers of *[kanbun](/wiki/Kanbun "Kanbun")*—the Japanese term for Literary Chinese writing—began utilizing a system of reading techniques and annotations called *kundoku*. When reading, Japanese speakers would adapt the syntax and vocabulary of Literary Chinese texts to reflect their Japanese-language equivalents. Writing essentially involved the inverse of this process, and resulted in ordinary Literary Chinese.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202088-154) When adapted to write Japanese, characters were used to represent both [Sino-Japanese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Japanese_vocabulary "Sino-Japanese vocabulary") loaned from Chinese, as well as the corresponding native synonyms. Most kanji were subject to both borrowing processes, and as a result have both Sino-Japanese and native readings, known as *[on'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi")* and *[kun'yomi](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi")* respectively. Moreover, kanji may have multiple readings of either kind. Distinct classes of *on'yomi* were borrowed into Japanese at different points in time from different varieties of Chinese.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoulmas1991122–129-155)

The [Japanese writing system](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system") is a mixed script, and has also incorporated syllabaries called *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* to represent phonetic units called *[moras](/wiki/Mora_(linguistics) "Mora (linguistics)")*, rather than morphemes. Prior to the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") (1868–1912), writers used certain kanji to represent their sound values instead, in a system known as *[man'yōgana](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dgana "Man'yōgana")*. Starting in the 9th century, specific *man'yōgana* were graphically simplified to create two distinct syllabaries called *[hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana")* and *[katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")*, which slowly replaced the earlier convention. Modern Japanese retains the use of kanji to represent most [word stems](/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem"), while *kana* syllabograms are generally used for grammatical affixes, particles, and loanwords. The forms of *hiragana* and *katakana* are visually distinct from one another, owing in large part to different methods of simplification—*katakana* were derived from smaller components of each *man'yōgana*, while *hiragana* were derived from the cursive forms of *man'yōgana* in their entirety. In addition, the *hiragana* and *katakana* for some moras were derived from different *man'yōgana*.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoulmas1991129–132-156) Characters invented for Japanese-language use are called *[kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji")*. The methods employed to create *kokuji* are equivalent to those used by Chinese-original characters, though most are ideographic compounds. For example, 峠 (*tōge*; 'mountain pass') is a compound *kokuji* composed of 山 ('mountain'), 上 ('above'), and 下 ('below').[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019192–196-157)

While characters used to write Chinese are monosyllabic, many kanji have multi-syllable readings. For example, the kanji 刀 has a native *kun'yomi* reading of *katana*. In different contexts, it can also be read with the *on'yomi* reading *tō*, such as in the Chinese loanword 日本刀 (*nihontō*; 'Japanese sword'), with a pronunciation corresponding to that in Chinese at the time of borrowing. Prior to the universal adoption of *katakana*, loanwords were typically written with unrelated kanji with *on'yomi* readings matching the syllables in the loanword. These spellings are called *[ateji](/wiki/Ateji "Ateji")*—for example, 亜米利加 (*Amerika*) was the *ateji* spelling of 'America', now rendered as アメリカ. As opposed to *man'yōgana* used solely for their pronunciation, *ateji* still corresponded to specific Japanese words. Some are still in use, with the official list of [*jōyō* kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji") including 106 *ateji* readings.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014275–279-158)

### Korean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Korean")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Chusa-Silsa_gusijang.jpg/250px-Chusa-Silsa_gusijang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chusa-Silsa_gusijang.jpg)

Literary Chinese calligraphy by the 17th- and 18th-century Korean artist and scholar [Kim Chŏnghŭi](/wiki/Kim_Ch%C5%8Fngh%C5%ADi "Kim Chŏnghŭi")

In Korean, Chinese characters are referred to as *[hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja")*. Literary Chinese may have been written in Korea as early as the 2nd century BCE. During Korea's [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea") period (57 BCE – 668 CE), characters were also used to write *[idu](/wiki/Idu_script "Idu script")*, a form of Korean-language literature that mostly made use of [Sino-Korean vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Korean_vocabulary "Sino-Korean vocabulary"). During the [Goryeo](/wiki/Goryeo "Goryeo") period (918–1392), Korean writers developed a system of phonetic annotations for Literary Chinese called *[gugyeol](/wiki/Gugyeol "Gugyeol")*, comparable to *kundoku* in Japan, though it only entered widespread use during the later [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon") period (1392–1897).[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi202078–80-159) While the [hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") alphabet was invented by the Joseon king [Sejong](/wiki/Sejong "Sejong") in 1443, it was not adopted by the Korean literati and was relegated to use in glosses for Literary Chinese texts until the late 19th century.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFischer2004189–194-160)

Much of the Korean lexicon consists of Chinese loanwords, especially technical and academic vocabulary.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199749TaylorTaylor2014435-161) While hanja were usually only used to write this Sino-Korean vocabulary, there is evidence that vernacular readings were sometimes used.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki2018168-138) Compared to the other written vernaculars, very few characters were invented to write Korean words; these are called *[gukja](/wiki/Gukja "Gukja")*.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201988,_102-162) During the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Korean was written either using a mixed script of hangul and hanja, or only using hangul.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019112–113Hannas199760–61-163) Following the end of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan")'s occupation of Korea in 1945, the total replacement of hanja with hangul was advocated throughout the country as part of a broader "purification movement" of the national language and culture.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199764–66-164) However, due to the lack of tones in spoken Korean, there are many Sino-Korean words that are homophones with identical hangul spellings. For example, the phonetic dictionary entry for 기사 (*gisa*) yields more than 30 different entries. This ambiguity had historically been resolved by also including the associated hanja. While still sometimes used for Sino-Korean vocabulary, it is much rarer for native Korean words to be written using hanja.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198879-165) When learning new characters, Korean students are instructed to associate each one with both its Sino-Korean pronunciation, as well as a native Korean synonym.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel201975–82-166) Examples include:

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Native translation | Sino-Korean |
| [水](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B4#Korean "wikt:水") | 물; *mul* | 수; *su* | 'water' |
| [人](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%BA#Korean "wikt:人") | 사람; *saram* | 인; *in* | 'person' |
| [大](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7#Korean "wikt:大") | 큰; *keun* | 대; *dae* | 'big' |
| [小](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B0%8F#Korean "wikt:小") | 작을; *jakeul* | 소; *so* | 'small' |
| [下](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8B#Korean "wikt:下") | 아래; *arae* | 하; *ha* | 'down' |
| [父](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%88%B6#Korean "wikt:父") | 아비; *abi* | 부; *bu* | 'father' |

### Vietnamese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Vietnamese")]

See also: [Literary Chinese in Vietnam](/wiki/Literary_Chinese_in_Vietnam "Literary Chinese in Vietnam")

[![The characters ｢𤾓𢆥𥪞𡎝𠊛些．𡨸才𡨸命窖𱺵恄𠑬𠑬．｣ corresponding to "Trăm năm trong cõi người ta. Chữ Tài chữ Mệnh khéo là ghét nhau." in the Vietnamese alphabet](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Tale_of_Kieu_parallel_text.svg/330px-Tale_of_Kieu_parallel_text.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tale_of_Kieu_parallel_text.svg)

In Vietnamese, Chinese characters are referred to as *[chữ Hán](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_H%C3%A1n "Chữ Hán")* (𡨸漢), *chữ Nho* (𡨸儒; '[Confucian](/wiki/Confucian "Confucian") characters'), or *Hán tự* (漢字). Literary Chinese was used for all formal writing in Vietnam until the modern era,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019124–126Kin2021XI-167) having first acquired official status in 1010. Literary Chinese written by Vietnamese authors is first attested in the late 10th century, though the local practice of writing is likely several centuries older.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199773-168) Characters used to write Vietnamese called *[chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm")* (𡨸喃) are first attested in an inscription dated to 1209 made at the site of a pagoda.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197723–24-169) A mature chữ Nôm script had likely emerged by the 13th century, and was initially used to record Vietnamese folk literature. Some chữ Nôm characters are phono-semantic compounds corresponding to spoken Vietnamese syllables.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki201863-170) Another technique with no equivalent in China created chữ Nôm compounds using two phonetic components. This was done because Vietnamese phonology included consonant clusters not found in Chinese, and were thus poorly approximated by the sound values of borrowed characters. Compounds used components with two distinct consonant sounds to specify the cluster, e.g. 𢁋 (*blăng*;[[d]](#cite_note-172) 'Moon') was created as a compound of 巴 (*ba*) and 陵 (*lăng*).[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019145,_150-173) As a system, chữ Nôm was highly complex, and the literacy rate among the Vietnamese population never exceeded 5%.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197719-174) Both Literary Chinese and chữ Nôm fell out of use during the French colonial period, and were gradually replaced by the Latin-based [Vietnamese alphabet](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet"). Following the end of colonial rule in 1954, the Vietnamese alphabet has been sole official writing system in Vietnam, and is used exclusively in Vietnamese-language media.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoulmas1991113–115Hannas199773,_84–87-175)

### Other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Other languages")]

Several minority languages of [South](/wiki/South_China "South China") and [Southwestern China](/wiki/Southwestern_China "Southwestern China") have been written with scripts using both borrowed and locally created characters. The most well-documented of these is the [sawndip](/wiki/Sawndip "Sawndip") script for the [Zhuang languages](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages") of [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"). While little is known about its early development, a tradition of vernacular Zhuang writing likely first emerged during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907). Modern scholarship characterizes sawndip writing as a network of regional traditions that have mutually influenced one another while maintaining their local characteristics.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019239–240-176) Like Vietnamese, some invented Zhuang characters are phonetic–phonetic compounds, though not primarily ones intended to describe consonant clusters.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019251–252-177) Despite the Chinese government encouraging its replacement with a Latin-based [Zhuang alphabet](/wiki/Zhuang_alphabet "Zhuang alphabet"), sawndip remains in use.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019231,_234–235Zhou2003140–142,_151-178) Other non-Sinitic [languages of China](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China") historically written with Chinese characters include [Miao](/wiki/Hmongic_languages "Hmongic languages"), [Yao](/wiki/Mienic_languages "Mienic languages"), [Bouyei](/wiki/Bouyei_language#Ancient_Bouyei_script "Bouyei language"), [Bai](/wiki/Bai_language "Bai language"), and [Hani](/wiki/Hani_language "Hani language"); each of these are now written with Latin-based alphabets designed for use with each language.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou1991Zhou2003139-179)

### Graphically derived scripts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Graphically derived scripts")]

See also: [Transcription into Chinese characters](/wiki/Transcription_into_Chinese_characters "Transcription into Chinese characters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/SecretHistoryMongols1908.jpg/250px-SecretHistoryMongols1908.jpg)](/wiki/File:SecretHistoryMongols1908.jpg)

Title page for a 1908 edition of the 13th-century *[Secret History of the Mongols](/wiki/Secret_History_of_the_Mongols "Secret History of the Mongols")*, which uses Chinese characters to transcribe Mongolian and provides glosses to the right of each column

Between the 10th and 13th centuries, dynasties founded by non-Han peoples in northern China also created scripts for their languages that were inspired by Chinese characters, but did not use them directly—these included the [Khitan large script](/wiki/Khitan_large_script "Khitan large script"), [Khitan small script](/wiki/Khitan_small_script "Khitan small script"), [Tangut script](/wiki/Tangut_script "Tangut script"), and [Jurchen script](/wiki/Jurchen_script "Jurchen script").[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou1991-180) This has occurred in other contexts as well: [Nüshu](/wiki/N%C3%BCshu "Nüshu") was a script used by [Yao](/wiki/Yao_people_(Asia) "Yao people (Asia)") women to write the [Xiangnan Tuhua](/wiki/Xiangnan_Tuhua "Xiangnan Tuhua") language,[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhao1998-181) and [bopomofo](/wiki/Bopomofo "Bopomofo") (注音符号; 注音符號; *zhùyīn fúhào*) is a [semi-syllabary](/wiki/Semi-syllabary "Semi-syllabary") first invented in 1907[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuzuoğlu202371-182) to represent the sounds of Standard Chinese;[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1984242TaylorTaylor201414Li2020123-183) both use forms graphically derived from Chinese characters. Other scripts within China that have adapted some characters but are otherwise distinct include the [Geba syllabary](/wiki/Geba_syllabary "Geba syllabary") used to write the [Naxi language](/wiki/Naxi_language "Naxi language"), [the script](/wiki/Sui_script "Sui script") for the [Sui language](/wiki/Sui_language "Sui language"), [the script](/wiki/Yi_script "Yi script") for the [Yi languages](/wiki/Yi_languages "Yi languages"), and the syllabary for the [Lisu language](/wiki/Lisu_language "Lisu language").[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou1991-180)

Chinese characters have also been repurposed phonetically to transcribe the sounds of non-Chinese languages. For example, the only manuscripts of the 13th-century *[Secret History of the Mongols](/wiki/Secret_History_of_the_Mongols "Secret History of the Mongols")* that have survived from the medieval era use characters in this manner to write the [Mongolian language](/wiki/Mongolian_language "Mongolian language").[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHung1951481-184)

## Literacy and lexicography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Literacy and lexicography")]

Further information: [Chinese character education](/wiki/Chinese_character_education "Chinese character education") and [Literacy in China](/wiki/Literacy_in_China "Literacy in China")

The memorization of thousands of different characters is required to achieve literacy in languages written with them, in contrast to the relatively small inventory of graphemes used in phonetic writing.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemattè20228TaylorTaylor2014110–111-185) Historically, character literacy was often acquired via Chinese primers like the 6th-century *[Thousand Character Classic](/wiki/Thousand_Character_Classic "Thousand Character Classic")* and 13th-century *[Three Character Classic](/wiki/Three_Character_Classic "Three Character Classic")*,[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki2018273–277-186) as well as surname dictionaries like the Song-era *[Hundred Family Surnames](/wiki/Hundred_Family_Surnames "Hundred Family Surnames")*.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng200855–58-187) Studies of Chinese-language literacy suggest that literate individuals generally have an [active vocabulary](/wiki/Active_vocabulary "Active vocabulary") of three to four thousand characters; for specialists in fields like literature or history, this figure may be between five and six thousand.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198873-188)

### Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

Main article: [Chinese dictionary](/wiki/Chinese_dictionary "Chinese dictionary")

[![天地玄黃](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Chenzihmyon_typefaces.svg/250px-Chenzihmyon_typefaces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chenzihmyon_typefaces.svg)

The first four characters of the 6th-century *[Thousand Character Classic](/wiki/Thousand_Character_Classic "Thousand Character Classic")* in different styles. From right to left: seal script, clerical script, regular script, Song type, and sans-serif type.

According to analyses of mainland Chinese, Taiwanese, Hong Kong, Japanese, and Korean sources, the total number of characters in the modern lexicon is around 15000.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESu201447,_51-189) Dozens of schemes have been devised for indexing Chinese characters and arranging them in dictionaries, though relatively few have achieved widespread use. Characters may be ordered according to methods based on their meaning, visual structure, or pronunciation.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESu2014183NeedhamHarbsmeier199865–66-190)

The **[Erya](/wiki/Erya "Erya")** (c. 3rd century BCE) organized the Chinese lexicon into 19 sections according to character meaning, with 3 dealing with everyday vocabulary, and each of the remaining 16 dedicated to specialized vocabulary related to a specific topic.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEXue1982152–153Demattè202237-191) The **Shuowen Jiezi** (c. 100 CE) introduced what would ultimately become the predominant method of organization used by later character dictionaries, whereby characters are grouped according to certain visually prominent components called *[radicals](/wiki/Chinese_character_radicals "Chinese character radicals")* (部首; *bùshǒu*; 'section headers'). The **Shuowen Jiezi** used a system of 540 radicals, while subsequent dictionaries have generally used fewer.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng2008100–103,_203-192) The set of 214 [Kangxi radicals](/wiki/Kangxi_radicals "Kangxi radicals") was popularized by the *[Kangxi Dictionary](/wiki/Kangxi_Dictionary "Kangxi Dictionary")* (1716), but originally appeared in the earlier **[Zihui](/wiki/Zihui "Zihui")** (1615).[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200388Norman1988170–172NeedhamHarbsmeier199879–80-193) Character dictionaries have historically been indexed using [radical-and-stroke sorting](/wiki/Radical-and-stroke_sorting "Radical-and-stroke sorting"), where characters are grouped by radical and [sorted within each group by stroke number](/wiki/Stroke-based_sorting "Stroke-based sorting"). Some modern dictionaries arrange character entries alphabetically according to their pinyin spelling, while also providing a traditional radical-based index.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYongPeng2008145,_400–401-194)

Before the invention of romanization systems for Chinese, the pronunciation of characters was transmitted via [rhyme dictionaries](/wiki/Rhyme_dictionaries "Rhyme dictionaries"). These used the *[fanqie](/wiki/Fanqie "Fanqie")* (反切; 'reverse cut') method, where each entry lists a common character with the same [initial sound](/wiki/Syllable#Chinese_model "Syllable") as the character in question, alongside one with the same final sound.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198827–28-195)

### Neurolinguistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Neurolinguistics")]

Using [functional magnetic resonance imaging](/wiki/Functional_magnetic_resonance_imaging "Functional magnetic resonance imaging") (fMRI), [neurolinguists](/wiki/Neurolinguist "Neurolinguist") have studied the brain activity associated with literacy. Compared to phonetic systems, reading and writing with characters involves additional areas of the brain—including those associated with visual processing.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemattè20229-196) While the level of memorization required for character literacy is significant, identification of the phonetic and semantic components in compounds—which constitute the vast majority of characters—also plays a key role in reading comprehension. The ease of recognition for a given character is impacted by how regular the positioning of its components is, as well as how reliable its phonetic component is in indicating a specific pronunciation.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2015b-197) Moreover, due to the high level of homophony in Chinese languages and the more irregular correspondences between writing and the sounds of speech, it has been suggested that knowledge of orthography plays a greater role in speech recognition for literate Chinese speakers.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2015aThe_Brain_Network_for_Chinese_Language_Processing-198)

[Developmental dyslexia](/wiki/Developmental_dyslexia "Developmental dyslexia") in readers of character-based languages appears to involve independent visuospatial and phonological disorders co-occurring. This seems to be a distinct phenomenon from dyslexia as experienced with phonetic orthographies, which can result from only one of the aforementioned disorders.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcBrideTongMo2015688–690Ho2015TaylorTaylor2014150–151,_346–349,_393–394-199)

## Reform and standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Reform and standardization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/ROC24_SC1.jpg/250px-ROC24_SC1.jpg)](/wiki/File:ROC24_SC1.jpg)

The first official list of simplified character forms, published in 1935 and including 324 characters[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999153-200)

Attempts to reform and standardize the use of characters—including aspects of form, stroke order, and pronunciation—have been undertaken by states throughout history. Thousands of [simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_characters "Simplified characters") were standardized and adopted in mainland China during the 1950s and 1960s, with most either already existing as common variants, or being produced via the systematic simplification of their components.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200360–67-201) After World War II, the Japanese government also simplified hundreds of character forms, including some simplifications distinct from those adopted in China.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014117–118-202) Orthodox forms that have not undergone simplification are referred to as *[traditional characters](/wiki/Traditional_characters "Traditional characters")*. Across Chinese-speaking polities, mainland China, Malaysia, and Singapore use simplified characters, while Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Macau use traditional characters.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020136-203) In general, Chinese and Japanese readers can successfully identify characters from all three standards.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang2016171-204)

Prior to the 20th century, reforms were generally conservative and sought to reduce the use of simplified variants.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQiu2000404-205) During the late 19th and early 20th centuries, an increasing number of intellectuals in China came to see both the Chinese writing system and the lack of a national spoken dialect as serious impediments to achieving the mass literacy and mutual intelligibility required for the country's successful modernization. Many began advocating for the replacement of Literary Chinese with a written language that more closely reflected speech, as well as for a mass simplification of character forms, or even the total [replacement of characters with an alphabet](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") tailored to a specific spoken variety. In 1909, the educator and linguist [Lufei Kui](/wiki/Lufei_Kui "Lufei Kui") formally proposed the adoption of simplified characters in education for the first time.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou2003xvii–xixLi2020136-206)

In 1911, the [Xinhai Revolution](/wiki/Xinhai_Revolution "Xinhai Revolution") toppled the Qing dynasty, and resulted in the establishment of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") the following year. The early Republican era (1912–1949) was characterized by growing social and political discontent that erupted into the 1919 [May Fourth Movement](/wiki/May_Fourth_Movement "May Fourth Movement"), catalysing the replacement of Literary Chinese with [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") over the subsequent decades. Alongside the corresponding spoken variety of [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), this written vernacular was promoted by intellectuals and writers such as [Lu Xun](/wiki/Lu_Xun "Lu Xun") and [Hu Shih](/wiki/Hu_Shih "Hu Shih").[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou2003xviii–xix-207) It was based on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") of [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"),[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197211–13-208) as well as on the existing body of vernacular literature authored over the preceding centuries, which included [classic novels](/wiki/Classic_Chinese_Novels "Classic Chinese Novels") such as *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")* (c. 1592) and *[Dream of the Red Chamber](/wiki/Dream_of_the_Red_Chamber "Dream of the Red Chamber")* (mid-18th century).[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhong2019113–114Chen199970–74,_80–82-209) At this time, character simplification and phonetic writing were being discussed within both the ruling [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) party, as well as the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP). In 1935, the Republican government published the first official list of simplified characters, comprising 324 forms collated by [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University") professor [Qian Xuantong](/wiki/Qian_Xuantong "Qian Xuantong"). However, strong opposition within the party resulted in the list being rescinded in 1936.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999150–153-210)

### People's Republic of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: People's Republic of China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/23/%E5%80%91-order.gif)](/wiki/File:%E5%80%91-order.gif)

Traditional (們)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3b/%E4%BB%AC-order.gif)](/wiki/File:%E4%BB%AC-order.gif)

Simplified (们)

Comparison between character forms, showing systematic simplification of the component ⾨ ('gate')

The project of script reform in China was ultimately inherited by the Communists, who resumed work following the [proclamation of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") in 1949. In 1951, Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") ordered the formation of a Script Reform Committee, with subgroups investigating both simplification and alphabetization. The simplification subgroup began surveying and collating simplified forms the following year,[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBökset200626-211) ultimately publishing a [draft scheme of simplified characters and components](/wiki/Chinese_Character_Simplification_Scheme "Chinese Character Simplification Scheme") in 1956. In 1958, Zhou Enlai announced the government's intent to focus on simplification, as opposed to replacing characters with [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"), which had been introduced earlier that year.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhong2019157–158-212) The 1956 scheme was largely ratified by a [revised list of 2235 characters](/wiki/General_List_of_Simplified_Chinese_Characters "General List of Simplified Chinese Characters") promulgated in 1964.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020142-213) The majority of these characters were drawn from conventional abbreviations or ancient forms with fewer strokes.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999154–156-214) The committee also sought to reduce the total number of characters in use by merging some forms together.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999154–156-214) For example, 雲 ('cloud') was written as 云 in oracle bone script. The simpler form remained in use as a loangraph meaning 'to say'; it was replaced in its original sense of 'cloud' with a form that added a semantic ⾬ ('rain') component. The simplified forms of these two characters have been merged into 云.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200363-215)

A [second round of simplified characters](/wiki/Second_round_of_simplified_characters "Second round of simplified characters") was promulgated in 1977, but was poorly received by the public and quickly fell out of official use. It was ultimately formally rescinded in 1986.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999155–156-216) The second-round simplifications were unpopular in large part because most of the forms were completely new, in contrast to the familiar variants comprising the majority of the first round.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999159–160-217) With the rescission of the second round, work toward further character simplification largely came to an end.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999196–197-218) The *Chart of Generally Utilized Characters of Modern Chinese* was published in 1988 and included 7000 simplified and unsimplified characters. Of these, half were also included in the revised *[List of Commonly Used Characters in Modern Chinese](/wiki/List_of_Commonly_Used_Characters_in_Modern_Chinese "List of Commonly Used Characters in Modern Chinese")*, which specified 2500 common characters and 1000 less common characters.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhou200379Chen1999136-219) In 2013, the *[List of Commonly Used Standard Chinese Characters](/wiki/List_of_Commonly_Used_Standard_Chinese_Characters "List of Commonly Used Standard Chinese Characters")* was published as a revision of the 1988 lists; it includes a total of 8105 characters.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020145–146-220)

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Japanese script reform](/wiki/Japanese_script_reform "Japanese script reform")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/CJK_%E6%AC%A1_glyph_variants.svg/250px-CJK_%E6%AC%A1_glyph_variants.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CJK_%E6%AC%A1_glyph_variants.svg)

Regional forms of the character 次 in the Noto Serif typeface family. From left to right: forms used in mainland China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong (top), and in Japan and Korea (bottom)

After World War II, the Japanese government instituted its own program of orthographic reforms. Some characters were assigned simplified forms called *[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")*; the older forms were then labelled *[kyūjitai](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABjitai "Kyūjitai")*. Inconsistent use of different variant forms was discouraged, and lists of characters to be taught to students at each grade level were developed. The first of these was the 1850-character [*tōyō* kanji](/wiki/T%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Tōyō kanji") list published in 1946, later replaced by the 1945-character [*jōyō* kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji") list in 1981. In 2010, the *jōyō* kanji were expanded to include a total of 2136 characters.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014275-221)[[211]](#cite_note-222) The Japanese government restricts characters that may be used in names to the *jōyō* kanji, plus an additional list of 983 [*jinmeiyō* kanji](/wiki/Jinmeiy%C5%8D_kanji "Jinmeiyō kanji") whose use are historically prevalent in names.[[212]](#cite_note-223)[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELunde200882–84-224)

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: South Korea")]

Hanja are still used in South Korea, though not to the extent that kanji are used in Japan. In general, there is a trend toward the exclusive use of hangul in ordinary contexts.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199748-225) Characters remain in use in place names, newspapers, and to disambiguate homophones. They are also used in the practice of calligraphy. Use of hanja in education is politically contentious, with official policy regarding the prominence of hanja in curricula having vacillated since the country's independence.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199765–66,_69–72-226)[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChooO'Grady1996ix-227) Some support the total abandonment of hanja, while others advocate an increase in use to levels previously seen during the 1970s and 1980s. Students in grades 7–12 are presently taught with a principal focus on simple recognition and attaining sufficient literacy to read a newspaper.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFischer2004189–194-160) The South Korean Ministry of Education published the *[Basic Hanja for Educational Use](/wiki/Basic_Hanja_for_Educational_Use "Basic Hanja for Educational Use")* in 1972, which specified 1800 characters meant to be learned by secondary school students.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELunde200884-228) In 1991, the [Supreme Court of Korea](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Korea "Supreme Court of Korea") published the *Table of Hanja for Use in Personal Names* (인명용 한자; *Inmyeong-yong Hanja*), which initially included 2854 characters.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014179-229) The list has been expanded several times since; as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit), it includes 8319 characters.[[219]](#cite_note-230)

### North Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: North Korea")]

In the years following its establishment, the North Korean government sought to eliminate the use of hanja in standard writing; by 1949, characters had been almost entirely replaced with hangul in North Korean publications.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019113Hannas199766–67-231) While mostly unused in writing, hanja remain an important part of North Korean education. A 1971 textbook for university history departments contained 3323 distinct characters, and in the 1990s North Korean schoolchildren were still expected to learn 2000 characters.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199767–68-232) A 2013 textbook appears to integrate the use of hanja in secondary school education.[[222]](#cite_note-233) It has been estimated that North Korean students learn around 3000 hanja by the time they graduate university.[[223]](#cite_note-234)

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

The *[Chart of Standard Forms of Common National Characters](/wiki/Chart_of_Standard_Forms_of_Common_National_Characters "Chart of Standard Forms of Common National Characters")* was published by Taiwan's Ministry of Education in 1982, and lists 4808 traditional characters.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELunde200881-235) The Ministry of Education also compiles dictionaries of characters used in [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") and [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese").[[H]](#cite_note-236)

### Other regional standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Other regional standards")]

Singapore's Ministry of Education promulgated three successive rounds of simplifications. The first round in 1969 included 502 simplified characters, and the second round in 1974 included 2287 simplified characters—including 49 that differed from those in the PRC, which were ultimately removed in the final round in 1976. In 1993, Singapore adopted the revisions made in mainland China in 1986.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShangZhao2017320-237)

The Hong Kong Education and Manpower Bureau's *[List of Graphemes of Commonly-Used Chinese Characters](/wiki/List_of_Graphemes_of_Commonly-Used_Chinese_Characters "List of Graphemes of Commonly-Used Chinese Characters")* includes 4762 traditional characters used in elementary and junior secondary education.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999161-238)
